narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put considerable effort into your application, including your LSAT score of 25 and middle-income family background. The admissions model evaluated your profile against patterns from thousands of past students, predicting a 40% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger projected odds of passing. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on how similar profiles have fared historically. The three most influential factors in this decision were your undergraduate GPA, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the typical 5.5 average), and your family's middle income bracket. Your GPA stood out as the strongest positive force, pushing the prediction toward bar passage because it's close to the average of 3.21 seen in successful students, showing you have a competitive academic foundation. In contrast, your low Year 3 decile was the top driver toward a failure prediction, as it signals performance lagging behind most peers in that critical later stage of schooling. Your middle family income provided a moderate helpful nudge toward success, likely reflecting stability that aligns with passers, though not as strongly as your GPA. Compared to typical admitted students, your LSAT is notably lower than the average 36.7, and your early Year 1 decile of 2 also trails the norm, which may have compounded the overall risk in the model's view. While full-time status is common and didn't sway things much, these elements together tipped the scales. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, strengthening your later-year grades if possible through additional coursework, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience and dedication as a Black man navigating these challenges. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and succeed on later attempts—your GPA shows you have the capability, so targeted preparation could open new doors soon. Keep pushing; this is just one data point, not your whole story.
